# Copilot Instructions

## Build, test, and lint commands

- `bundle exec rake spec` - primary test task defined in `Rakefile` and used by CI in `.travis.yml`
- `bundle exec rspec spec/rubocop/formatter/checkstyle_formatter_spec.rb` - run the only spec file directly
- `bundle exec rspec spec/rubocop/formatter/checkstyle_formatter_spec.rb:67` - run a single example by line number
- `bundle exec rubocop` - lint the project using `.rubocop.yml`
- `BUNDLE_GEMFILE=gemfiles/rubocop_0.54.0.gemfile bundle exec rake spec` - run the spec suite against one Appraisal-generated RuboCop version; swap the gemfile to check other supported RuboCop releases

The dependency toolchain is split across eras: `.travis.yml` targets Ruby 2.2-2.5-era environments and the gemspec still declares `bundler ~> 1.3`, while `.tool-versions` points at Ruby 3.2.2 and `Gemfile.lock` was generated by Bundler 2.5.5. If setup fails, check whether the Ruby/Bundler combination matches the part of the matrix you are trying to exercise before changing formatter code.

## High-level architecture

- `lib/rubocop/formatter/checkstyle_formatter.rb` is the product: a `RuboCop::Formatter::BaseFormatter` subclass that builds one `REXML::Document` across RuboCop's formatter lifecycle (`started`, `file_finished`, `finished`).
- `started` creates the XML document and root `<checkstyle>` node, `file_finished` appends a `<file>` node per inspected file, and `finished` writes the full XML to the formatter output stream.
- Each offense is converted into an `<error>` element with `line`, `column`, `severity`, `message`, and `source`. The `source` value is always prefixed with `com.puppycrawl.tools.checkstyle.` because the formatter is meant to feed Checkstyle-compatible consumers such as Jenkins.
- Path handling is compatibility-aware: when RuboCop exposes `PathUtil`, the formatter emits relative paths by default via `relative_path`; setting `RUBOCOP_CHECKSTYLE_FORMATTER_ABSOLUTE_PATH` keeps absolute paths instead.
- The formatter deliberately supports multiple RuboCop API generations. It aliases `Rubocop` to `RuboCop` for older releases and guards optional behavior with `defined?` checks instead of assuming one RuboCop version.
- `spec/rubocop/formatter/checkstyle_formatter_spec.rb` tests the XML contract directly by constructing offenses in memory instead of shelling out to the RuboCop CLI. `Appraisals`, `gemfiles/*.gemfile`, and `.travis.yml` together define the multi-version RuboCop compatibility matrix those specs are meant to protect.

## Key conventions

- Backward compatibility is a first-class goal. When changing formatter or spec code, prefer runtime feature detection (`defined?`, `respond_to?`, method parameter inspection) over explicit RuboCop version branches.
- Keep the legacy `offence`/`add_offence` spellings in compatibility code. They are intentional support for older RuboCop APIs, not naming drift to clean up.
- Treat severity mapping as part of the formatter's public output contract: `fatal`/`error` -> `error`, `warning` -> `warning`, `convention`/`refactor` -> `info`, everything else -> `warning`.
- Specs are focused contract tests, not end-to-end CLI tests. Follow the existing pattern of constructing synthetic offenses and asserting on generated XML when adding coverage.
